description.en: Southern Thailand's main commercial city, known for Kim Yong Market, Thai-Chinese and Malay-Muslim food, easy airport access, and quick side trips to Songkhla.
description.nl: De belangrijkste handelsstad van Zuid-Thailand, bekend om Kim Yong Market, Thai-Chinese en Maleis-moslimkeukens, vlotte luchthavenverbindingen en snelle uitstappen naar Songkhla.
description.ja: キムヨン市場、タイ中華とマレー系ムスリムの食文化、空港アクセス、ソンクラー方面への小旅行で知られる南タイの商業都市です。
description.ko: 킴용 마켓, 태국계 중국식과 말레이 무슬림 음식, 쉬운 공항 접근성, 송클라 방면 반일치기로 알려진 남부 태국의 상업 중심 도시입니다.
description.th: เมืองศูนย์กลางการค้าใหญ่ของภาคใต้ เด่นเรื่องตลาดกิมหยง อาหารไทยเชื้อสายจีนและมลายูมุสลิม การเดินทางสะดวก และต่อทริปไปเมืองสงขลาได้ง่าย
description.de: Die wichtigste Handelsstadt Südthailands, bekannt für den Kim Yong Market, thai-chinesische und malaiisch-muslimische Küche, gute Flughafenanbindung und schnelle Abstecher nach Songkhla.
description.fr: Le principal centre commercial du sud de la Thaïlande, connu pour Kim Yong Market, sa cuisine thaïe-chinoise et malaise musulmane, son accès aéroportuaire pratique et ses excursions faciles vers Songkhla.
description.ru: Главный коммерческий город юга Таиланда, известный рынком Kim Yong, тайско-китайской и малайско-мусульманской кухней, удобным аэропортом и быстрыми выездами в Сонгкхлу.
description.zh: 合艾是泰国南部的重要商业城市，以 Kim Yong Market、泰华与马来穆斯林饮食、便利机场连接和前往宋卡的短途行程而知名。

metaDescription.nl: Plan Hat Yai slimmer met Kim Yong Market, Khlong Hae-timing, Thai-Chinese en Maleis-moslimeten, sterke centrumhotels, luchthavenlogistiek en een realistische side trip naar Songkhla Old Town.
metaDescription.ja: キムヨン市場、คลองแห、南部らしい食文化、中心部ホテル、空港導線、ソンクラー旧市街への現実的な小旅行まで整理したハジャイ実用ガイドです。

metaDescription.ru: Планируйте Хатъяй лучше: Kim Yong Market, график Khlong Hae, тайско-китайская и малайско-мусульманская еда, сильные центральные отели, логистика аэропорта и реальная поездка в старый город Сонгкхлы.
metaDescription.zh: 从 Kim Yong Market、Khlong Hae 营业节奏、泰华与马来穆斯林美食，到市中心酒店、机场往返和宋卡老城短途，这份指南帮你更好规划合艾。

attractions.ja: ハジャイの人気観光スポット
attractions.th: สถานที่ท่องเที่ยวยอดนิยมในหาดใหญ่

enhanced_description:
Hat Yai works best when you stop comparing it to Thailand's beach destinations and start using it for what it is actually good at: markets, food, transport, shopping, and efficient southern-city planning. Official Songkhla and TAT material still frames Hat Yai as the commercial and transport center of the south, and that remains the most useful first principle. The city is busy, practical, and rarely polished in a resort sense, but that is exactly why it can be stronger than people expect.

Kim Yong Market and the surrounding downtown blocks explain Hat Yai better than almost any single attraction. This is where the city feels like a working trade hub rather than a staged tourist zone. Add the Thai-Chinese breakfast culture, Malay-Muslim food references from TAT's halal guide, and the evening-market layer from Khlong Hae or ASEAN Night Bazaar, and the city starts to make sense as a food-first stop rather than a checklist destination.

Hat Yai also gets better once you plan the surrounding pieces correctly. The municipal park and Wat Hat Yai Nai give the city a quick local-identity layer, Songkhla Old Town makes a realistic half-day or day trip, and the airport plus rail links make arrivals and departures easy. The mistake is treating Hat Yai as either a generic transit stop or a place where every mall and market deserves equal weight. It is better as a one- to three-night southern base with edited priorities: one strong market morning, one serious eating day, one evening market, and one carefully chosen side trip.
overview:
Hat Yai has two usable versions, and choosing between them shapes the whole stay. The first is downtown-first: stay near Lee Gardens Plaza, Kim Yong Market, and the central streets so you can walk to markets, food, and evening activity. The second is outer-center or airport-side: use a hotel such as Hatyai Signature, W3, Crystal, or Monkham when parking, quieter nights, or faster airport access matters more than being directly inside the older trading core. Most first-time visitors do better with the downtown version.

The city's attraction mix is practical rather than monumental. Official provincial material still points back to Hat Yai Municipal Park, Wat Hat Yai Nai, Kim Yong Market, Ton Nga Chang, and Khlong Hae, and that is the right scale to work with. Build one day around the city itself, one evening around a market, and one half-day around Songkhla or Ton Nga Chang if you have the time. Hat Yai is strongest when the itinerary stays edited and urban rather than trying to imitate Phuket, Bangkok, or a resort island.

hidden_gems.name: Use Kim Yong and Santisuk as one downtown block
hidden_gems.story: Official Hat Yai tourism material points to both Kim Yong and Santisuk, and they work best together rather than as separate headline stops. Kim Yong gives you the trading-market side, while the surrounding lanes handle souvenirs, dry goods, and practical city movement.
hidden_gems.how_to_find: Start early around Kim Yong, then keep walking instead of calling transport after the first market pass.
local_insights: Hat Yai is clearer on foot in the old commercial center than from inside a taxi.
Morning market time is more useful than another late-mall stop.

hidden_gems.name: Khlong Hae only works when you respect the schedule
hidden_gems.story: The official Songkhla city reference is unusually specific here: Khlong Hae opens Friday to Sunday from 15:00 to 21:00. That timing discipline matters because plenty of weak Hat Yai guides still talk about it as if it runs all day, every day.
hidden_gems.how_to_find: Use it as a late-afternoon or evening plan on the correct days, not as a flexible anytime attraction.
hidden_gems.best_time: Friday to Sunday, late afternoon into evening.
local_insights: This is better as a food-and-atmosphere visit than a shopping mission.
If you miss the operating window, swap to downtown markets instead of forcing the trip.

hidden_gems.name: Municipal Park is more useful than it first looks
hidden_gems.story: The park is not just a green break. Official Songkhla references frame it as Hat Yai's major public recreation area, and in practice it is one of the quickest ways to understand the city's hilltop orientation, Guan Yin area, and broader urban layout.
hidden_gems.how_to_find: Pair it with Wat Hat Yai Nai or a downtown evening market rather than giving it a full standalone day.
local_insights: The park helps Hat Yai feel geographically legible very quickly.
It is more rewarding when used for context and views than for box-ticking.

hidden_gems.name: Songkhla Old Town deserves an early start
hidden_gems.story: Old Town is one of the best nearby cultural extensions, but it works much better before the heat builds and before you let Hat Yai traffic eat half the day.
hidden_gems.how_to_find: Leave Hat Yai after breakfast, walk Old Town and a few heritage streets, then return for a Hat Yai dinner or night market.
local_insights: This is one of the cleanest ways to add heritage depth to a Hat Yai trip.
Use it as one focused side trip, not one stop among several rushed detours.

authentic_experiences.activity: Build one proper Hat Yai market morning
authentic_experiences.story: Kim Yong Market is not a tourist prop in official Songkhla material; it is a city symbol and everyday commercial anchor. Using the market seriously gives you the strongest first look at Hat Yai's identity.
authentic_experiences.cultural_significance: The downtown market zone shows Hat Yai as a border-trade city shaped by Thai, Chinese, and Malaysian-facing commerce.
authentic_experiences.how_to_participate: Go early, walk Kim Yong and the surrounding central lanes, then keep lunch nearby instead of relocating immediately.
insights: Morning is a better use of Hat Yai than sleeping late and starting in a mall.
The city becomes more coherent once you see how its market core actually functions.

authentic_experiences.activity: Split Hat Yai food between Thai-Chinese breakfast and Malay-Muslim meals
authentic_experiences.story: TAT's halal guide is useful because it makes clear that Hat Yai's food identity is not one-note. Fried chicken, dim sum, tea, halal kitchens, and market food all belong here.
authentic_experiences.cultural_significance: That mix reflects the city's southern border character and the overlap between Thai-Chinese trading history and Muslim food culture.
authentic_experiences.how_to_participate: Do one dim sum or breakfast stop, one halal or Muslim-leaning lunch, and one evening market instead of trying to force one signature dish to explain the whole city.
insights: Hat Yai is stronger as a multi-meal city than as a single famous-restaurant city.
Variety is the point here, not prestige dining.
authentic_experiences.cta_label: See the Hat Yai food guide

authentic_experiences.activity: Use the municipal park and Wat Hat Yai Nai as the city's local-identity layer
authentic_experiences.story: These two official staples are still worth keeping because they add a civic and religious dimension that the market-only version of Hat Yai can miss.
authentic_experiences.cultural_significance: The park shows public Hat Yai at leisure, while Wat Hat Yai Nai remains one of the clearest Buddhist landmarks in the urban area.
authentic_experiences.how_to_participate: Do one in the morning and the other later in the day, then return downtown for dinner.
insights: These are supporting stops, not reasons to overbuild the itinerary.
Together they make the city feel more complete.

authentic_experiences.activity: Treat Songkhla Old Town as Hat Yai's best cultural extension
authentic_experiences.story: If you want more than markets and shopping, Songkhla Old Town is the best nearby answer. It adds heritage streets, street art, museums, and a much stronger historical layer than Hat Yai itself can provide.
authentic_experiences.cultural_significance: This is the clearest way to add southern-port history and architecture to a Hat Yai-based trip.
authentic_experiences.how_to_participate: Go in the morning, keep the route selective, and return to Hat Yai for your evening food plan.
insights: This is a better side trip than trying to cram multiple minor stops into one day.
Hat Yai plus Songkhla is a stronger pairing than Hat Yai alone.
authentic_experiences.cta_label: See the Songkhla Old Town guide

foodie_adventures.dish: Decha-style fried chicken and southern Muslim comfort food
foodie_adventures.story: TAT's halal guide lists Decha Fried Chicken & Seafood directly, which makes it a stronger Hat Yai reference than generic scraped best-of lists. It fits the city because the meal sits at the overlap of fast local favorite, repeatable lunch stop, and border-city food culture.
foodie_adventures.where_to_find: Use it as a proper lunch or early dinner, not only as a snack stop between markets.
ordering_tips: Go with more than one item so the meal does not flatten into one fried dish.
This is a better use of Hat Yai than chasing imported cafe food.
foodie_adventures.cta_label: See the Hat Yai food guide

foodie_adventures.dish: Dim sum breakfast with a southern-city rhythm
foodie_adventures.location: Rafa Dimsum Halal and other breakfast-led stops
foodie_adventures.story: TAT's halal guide highlights Rafa Dimsum Halal, and that matters because it anchors Hat Yai's breakfast side in a primary source rather than travel-forum hearsay.
foodie_adventures.where_to_find: Use dim sum early, ideally before downtown market walking or a Songkhla departure.
ordering_tips: Breakfast works better than a late brunch if you want the city at full pace.
Dim sum is one of the easiest ways to understand Hat Yai's Thai-Chinese side.
foodie_adventures.cta_label: See the Hat Yai food guide

foodie_adventures.dish: Kim Yong snacks, dry goods, tea, and market grazing
foodie_adventures.story: Official Songkhla material treats Kim Yong as a city symbol and a source of inexpensive goods and souvenirs. That same market logic is why it works so well for snack-led food exploration.
foodie_adventures.where_to_find: Go in the morning and graze lightly while you walk, then keep lunch separate.
ordering_tips: Use the market for variety and pantry-style purchases more than for one long sit-down meal.
This is a better morning stop than a late-night one.

foodie_adventures.dish: Weekend floating-market food served in natural containers
foodie_adventures.story: The official Songkhla city reference specifically notes food sold from boats and on land, with many items served in coconut shells and other natural containers. That is exactly the durable detail worth keeping.
foodie_adventures.where_to_find: Friday to Sunday, late afternoon into evening.
ordering_tips: Do not save this for weekdays; the schedule is part of the planning.
Treat it as an evening food stop, not a lunch fallback.

local_insights.observation: Hat Yai is better as a city of zones than a city of landmarks.
local_insights.tip: Keep downtown markets, park-and-temple stops, and Songkhla side trips in separate blocks instead of trying to cross the region repeatedly in one day.
local_insights.surprise: The city feels more efficient once you stop expecting a single signature monument.

local_insights.observation: Downtown is still the easiest first base.
local_insights.tip: If this is your first Hat Yai stay, sleep near Lee Gardens, Kim Yong, or the central commercial area unless parking or airport timing is your main concern.
local_insights.surprise: A quieter hotel outside the center can be worse if you spend your whole trip paying to get back into town.

local_insights.tip: TAT's Hat Yai guidance warns that Malaysian holiday weekends can tighten room supply, so book central hotels earlier when demand spikes.
local_insights.surprise: Hat Yai demand is often driven more by regional weekend traffic than by long-haul tourism.

local_insights.observation: One side trip is enough on short stays.
local_insights.tip: Choose Songkhla Old Town or Ton Nga Chang if you only have two days; trying to add both usually weakens the city itself.
local_insights.surprise: Hat Yai becomes stronger once the day trip supports the city instead of replacing it.

seasonal_secrets.why: This is usually the easiest period for market walking, city heat management, and combining Hat Yai with Songkhla or other nearby detours.
local_festivals: Songkran brings major street activity around central Hat Yai roads.
Cross-border holiday weekends often create a busier city-center hotel and market atmosphere.
seasonal_foods: Weekend market food is strongest when Khlong Hae is open and the weather is dry enough to linger.
Hot-weather months make breakfast and evening food plans more comfortable than heavy midday walking.
insider_tips: In hotter months, do downtown in the morning and markets again after dark.
Book earlier if your trip lines up with Malaysian school or holiday traffic.

examples: Budget travelers do well with central guesthouses, dim sum breakfasts, market meals, and selective transport use.
Mid-range travelers usually benefit most from a strong central hotel and one or two better restaurant or hotel-dining choices.
Luxury spending in Hat Yai is more about hotel comfort and convenience than about destination-level resort experiences.
money_saving_tricks: Stay central if your plan is mostly markets and food, because repeated transport costs add up quickly.
Use one market meal and one local restaurant meal per day instead of defaulting to hotel dining every time.
Choose either Songkhla or Ton Nga Chang on short stays instead of paying for multiple long car trips.
hidden_costs: Traffic-time taxis and repeated short urban transfers can cost more than expected.
Airport-side hotels save time only if airport timing is actually central to your trip.

top_attractions.name: Kim Yong Market
top_attractions.description: The market that still explains Hat Yai best: city-symbol trading lanes, inexpensive goods, and a morning food-and-shopping rhythm that feels local-first.
highlights: Useful for snacks, dry goods, and souvenirs

top_attractions.name: Khlong Hae Floating Market
top_attractions.description: A worthwhile late-afternoon or evening plan when you respect the official Friday-to-Sunday schedule and go for food and atmosphere, not just photos.
highlights: Best evening market extension outside downtown

top_attractions.name: Hat Yai Municipal Park
top_attractions.description: A practical hilltop-and-civic stop that adds views, public-city context, and an easy break from the downtown commercial core.
highlights: Best paired with one other city sight

top_attractions.name: Wat Hat Yai Nai
top_attractions.description: One of Hat Yai's defining Buddhist landmarks and the easiest temple stop to add meaningful local context to a market-focused stay.

top_attractions.name: ASEAN Night Bazaar
top_attractions.description: A practical evening option if you want Hat Yai nightlife in market form rather than in bars or malls.

top_attractions.name: Songkhla Old Town
top_attractions.description: The best cultural side trip from Hat Yai if you want heritage streets, museums, and a much stronger architectural layer than Hat Yai itself can provide.
top_attractions.entrance_fee: Public streets free; museums and cafes set their own prices
highlights: Adds heritage depth to a city stay

top_restaurants.name: Decha Fried Chicken & Seafood
top_restaurants.description: A TAT-listed Hat Yai classic that gives the city a stronger food identity than generic review-driven lists.
highlights: Good lunch or early dinner anchor
Fits Hat Yai's southern border-city food mix
top_restaurants.cta_label: See the full Hat Yai food guide

top_restaurants.name: Kim Yong Market food lanes
top_restaurants.description: The city's best food-and-trade morning zone when you want Hat Yai to feel local and practical from the start.
highlights: Useful for snacks and takeaway buys

top_restaurants.name: Khlong Hae Floating Market
top_restaurants.description: A strong weekend-only food stop for travelers who want the floating-market side of Hat Yai without pretending it runs every day.
highlights: Food from boats and land stalls

top_restaurants.name: Rafa Dimsum Halal
top_restaurants.description: One of the clearest TAT-backed breakfast references if you want Hat Yai's morning dining rhythm instead of another generic cafe.
top_restaurants.cta_label: See the full Hat Yai food guide

top_restaurants.name: Sami Kitchen
top_restaurants.description: A useful TAT-listed local restaurant if you want a proper meal beyond the better-known market stops.
highlights: Useful sit-down alternative to pure market grazing
top_restaurants.cta_label: See the full Hat Yai food guide

top_restaurants.name: Salma Restaurant
top_restaurants.description: A reliable TAT-listed dining option when you want a straightforward city-center meal with local rather than tourist-first positioning.
top_restaurants.cta_label: See the full Hat Yai food guide

top_restaurants.name: Ginger Restaurant at Centara Hotel Hat Yai
top_restaurants.description: A sensible centrally located hotel-dining option if comfort and location matter more than chasing street-food atmosphere every meal.
highlights: Good fallback for weather or convenience
top_restaurants.cta_label: See the full Hat Yai food guide

top_restaurants.name: Signature Cafe & Restaurant
top_restaurants.description: An official in-house dining option that works well when you stay on the airport-side or outer-center side of Hat Yai.
top_restaurants.cta_label: See the full Hat Yai food guide

top_hotels.name: Lee Gardens Plaza Hotel
top_hotels.description: Still one of the clearest central stays if walkable market access and downtown convenience are the main point of the trip.
highlights: Walking distance to Kim Yong Market
top_hotels.cta_label: See the Hat Yai hotel guide

top_hotels.name: Centara Hotel Hat Yai
top_hotels.description: A well-located full-service city hotel linked to the shopping core, with stronger business and convenience logic than boutique charm.
highlights: 30 minutes from the airport per Centara
Good for central comfort and meetings
top_hotels.cta_label: See the Hat Yai hotel guide

top_hotels.name: New Season Square Hotel
top_hotels.description: A strong fit for travelers who want a newer downtown hotel close to shopping and night activity without leaving the center.
highlights: Useful alternative to the older central towers
top_hotels.cta_label: See the Hat Yai hotel guide

top_hotels.name: Crystal Hotel Hat Yai
top_hotels.description: A practical modern hotel if you want easier road access, shopping proximity, and airport convenience without relying on the older downtown core.
highlights: Pool, fitness, and dining on site
Good for drivers and business stays
top_hotels.cta_label: See the Hat Yai hotel guide

top_hotels.name: Monkham Village Hat Yai
top_hotels.description: A quieter boutique-style option on the Rajyindee side for travelers who want a calmer stay without moving too far from the urban core.
top_hotels.cta_label: See the Hat Yai hotel guide

top_hotels.name: W3 Hotel Hatyai
top_hotels.description: A practical stay in a quieter setting that still claims a five-minute run into downtown, making it useful for drivers and short urban stays.
top_hotels.cta_label: See the Hat Yai hotel guide

top_hotels.name: Hatyai Signature Hotel
top_hotels.description: A solid choice when airport access, parking, and a more self-contained hotel setup matter more than walking everywhere downtown.
highlights: Restaurant, pool, and fitness on site
top_hotels.cta_label: See the Hat Yai hotel guide

travel_tips: Use downtown for your first stay unless airport timing or parking matters more than walkable market access.
Do Kim Yong in the morning, not late, if you want Hat Yai's commercial core at full strength.
Treat Khlong Hae as a Friday-to-Sunday market only; if it is closed, pivot back to central Hat Yai instead of forcing the detour.
Keep Songkhla Old Town or Ton Nga Chang as a single deliberate side trip rather than stacking both on short stays.
Book earlier on Malaysian holiday weekends when central Hat Yai hotels fill faster.
Split meals between breakfast culture, halal and Muslim-friendly kitchens, and one evening market rather than repeating the same downtown pattern every day.

best_time_to_visit.weather: Usually the easiest stretch for market walking, side trips, and managing Hat Yai's heat and humidity.
best_time_to_visit.reasons: Best for first-time visits that want downtown walking, one market-heavy day, and one practical excursion to Songkhla or a park area.

budget_info.notes: Hat Yai stays cheaper than resort Thailand, but transport hops and central-hotel timing can shift the daily spend quickly.

thingsToDo: A good first Hat Yai stay needs less sightseeing ambition and more structure. Start with Kim Yong Market and the downtown trade core in the morning, use either Hat Yai Municipal Park or Wat Hat Yai Nai for civic and local context, and finish with ASEAN Night Bazaar or Khlong Hae depending on the day. If you have a second full day, choose Songkhla Old Town for heritage or Ton Nga Chang for nature, but not both unless you genuinely have extra time.
whereToStay: Downtown remains the most useful first base because Lee Gardens, Kim Yong, Santisuk, and much of Hat Yai's evening life still cluster there. Travelers who value quieter nights, parking, or airport access may do better on the Rajyindee, Kanchanawanit, or outer-center side, but those locations work best when you already know why you are giving up downtown walkability.

whereToEat.name: Decha Fried Chicken & Seafood
whereToEat.description: One of the clearest TAT-backed Hat Yai meal anchors for a real local lunch or dinner.

whereToEat.name: Kim Yong Market food lanes
whereToEat.description: The strongest morning grazing zone if you want Hat Yai's market identity to do the work.

whereToEat.name: Rafa Dimsum Halal
whereToEat.description: A breakfast-led stop that reflects Hat Yai's Thai-Chinese and Muslim crossover.

whereToEat.name: Khlong Hae Floating Market
whereToEat.description: Best for late-afternoon or evening eating when it is actually open.

whereToEat.name: Ginger Restaurant at Centara Hotel Hat Yai
whereToEat.description: A practical central comfort choice when weather or convenience outweighs street-food energy.

gettingAround: Central Hat Yai is walkable in short blocks around Lee Gardens, Kim Yong, and the older commercial core, but the wider city still works better with short taxi, tuk-tuk, songthaew, or hotel-car hops. Songkhla Old Town, Khlong Hae, the municipal park, and airport-side hotels all become easier once you accept that Hat Yai is a practical transport city rather than a compact heritage center.
bestTimeToVisit: Hat Yai is easiest from November to February, when walking the downtown markets, adding a Songkhla side trip, and handling the city's heat all become simpler. March to May can still work well if you shift more of the plan toward mornings and evenings, but the middle of the day gets heavier. The wetter months are still workable for food-led city trips, yet they are less ideal if Khlong Hae timing, park visits, or side trips are important. For most first Hat Yai visits, the cooler and drier season gives the cleanest balance.

budget.description: Budget Hat Yai usually means a simple central room, breakfast-led local food, and selective transport.

midrange.description: Mid-range travelers get the best value in Hat Yai, especially with a strong central or outer-center hotel and better meal flexibility.

luxury.description: Luxury spend goes further on comfort and convenience than on destination-style resort experiences.

safetyTips: Hat Yai is generally straightforward for short city stays, but the main practical risks are traffic, heavy rain, and poor route planning rather than nightlife drama. Use normal city precautions in markets, keep valuables close in crowded areas, and do not build rigid schedules around weekend-only attractions. If you are heading to Songkhla or Ton Nga Chang, leave with enough daylight to avoid turning a short side trip into a rushed return.

faq.question: Is Hat Yai worth more than a stopover?
faq.answer: Yes, if you use it for what it actually does well: markets, food, efficient transport, and one smart side trip. One or two nights is often enough to prove that it is more than a transit city.

faq.question: How many days do you need in Hat Yai?
faq.answer: Two nights is the easiest first-time rhythm. That gives you one real city day plus time for either Songkhla Old Town, Ton Nga Chang, or a slower market-and-food schedule.

faq.question: Should first-timers stay downtown?
faq.answer: Usually yes. Downtown keeps Kim Yong, Lee Gardens, and much of Hat Yai's practical food-and-shopping zone within easier reach. Stay outside the center only if you specifically want road access, airport convenience, or quieter nights.

faq.question: Can you do Songkhla Old Town from Hat Yai?
faq.answer: Yes. It is one of the most realistic and worthwhile side trips from Hat Yai, especially if you leave after breakfast and return for dinner back in the city.

practicalInfo.travelTimeFromAirport: Usually about 20 to 30 minutes to central Hat Yai.
localTransport: Taxi and tuk-tuk for direct city movements
Hotel-arranged transport for airport and Songkhla trips
Walking in the downtown commercial core
